figures.caption: Table 2: Detailed evaluation results on 5 math reasoning benchmarks. Among the non-base training settings within each model group, the best and second-best results are bolded and underlined, respectively. Under a fair setup of equal rollout budget, “Medium@8” achieves the best average performance for all three model settings. ↑ and ↓ respectively indicate improvements or degradations over the Full baseline under the same model and benchmark.
Table 3: Per-sample one-sample training results. Each row is one training run on a single replicated sample.
Figure 5: Per-split emergence of new reasoning features. Each panel plots the relative-to-early-mean ReasonScore trajectories of the emerging T-SAE features in one regime.
Figure 7: KL divergence DKL(πθ∥πref), Average advantages Ā and performance pass@1 between the policy model πθ and reference model πref on MATH and Zero-Variance MATH.

Figure 8: Each subplot tracks a different evaluation metric over 58 training steps. The first panel shows the average response length (in tokens); the remaining five panels report accuracy on AMC, Math 500, AIME 2024, Minerva Math, and OlympiadBench, respectively. The faint line denotes raw per-step values; the bold line is a 5-step moving average. Dashed horizontal lines mark the initial value at step 1.

Figure 11: Difficulty-specific T-SAE feature dynamics under one-sample RL. We track representative sparse features whose REASONSCORE changes differently when training on Easy, Medium, and Hard@8 samples. Easy samples mainly reinforce short-chain arithmetic reduction and existencerecognition features, medium samples strengthen explicit problem-solving operations, and hard samples amplify verbose connective or filler features rather than content-bearing reasoning operations. These patterns show that sample difficulty reshapes not only optimization dynamics but also the type of internal reasoning features reinforced by RLVR.

Figure 6: Token-level T-SAE feature dynamics along representative reasoning trajectories after RL on samples of different difficulty. The top row visualizes features suppressed by Hard@8 training, and the bottom row visualizes features reinforced by Hard@8 training. Hard samples tend to weaken existing reasoning features while amplifying more local or surface-level patterns.

Figure 1: Overall performance of GRPO across multiple benchmarks using Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B as the base model. We partition training samples into Easy, Medium, and Hard regimes, where Easy is defined as easy@8, Hard as hard@8, and Medium contains the remaining samples.

Figure 2: Difficulty curriculum for practical RLVR. We compare training on curriculum subsets defined by different rollout-based difficulty thresholds across three base models. Samples that are solvable within a limited rollout budget consistently yield stronger improvements than persistently unsolved samples, highlighting the importance of difficulty-aware data selection in RLVR.

Table 4: Distribution of the top-200 most frequent primary activating tokens across all 24,576 SAE and TSAE features, grouped by semantic category. #SAE and #TSAE indicate the total number of features whose highest-activation example activates on tokens in each category.

Table 5: Distribution of the top-200 most frequent primary activating tokens across all 24,576 SAE and TSAE features, grouped by semantic category. #SAE and #TSAE indicate the total number of features whose highest-activation example activates on tokens in each category.

Figure 9: Emergence of new reasoning capabilities under RLVR. (a) Trajectories of the 13 emerging T-SAE features across the 29 RL checkpoints (step_2–step_58), with the four highlighted features in saturated colour, the remaining nine in light grey, and the cohort mean as a black dashed line. All features start near zero and rise monotonically. (b)–(e) Per-feature trajectories for the four highlighted emerging features, annotated with the activating-token semantics and with their early/late mean ReasonScores (searly averaged over the first 5 checkpoints, slate over the last 5). Together they expose four qualitatively new reasoning behaviors—structured answer summarisation, structured problem decomposition, mathematical-expression construction, and active problem-solving strategy— that are not present in the base model and that RL training appears to construct rather than merely amplify.

Figure 3: One-sample RL performance on MATH-500 test subsets. For each training regime (easy@8, medium@8, and hard@8), we sample 10 individual problems, train on each problem separately, and evaluate on the MATH-500 easy@8 and medium@8 test subsets. The hard@8 test subset is omitted because all runs achieve 0 accuracy.
